speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five, interview with Ania Radek. We were speaking about the liberation. And you had said that you were in the house, and two American soldiers came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you escaped. And I wondered where you escaped to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I escaped first in the washroom. Then I escaped into the barn, where I hid under the rafters, way, way, way at the back. And later on, they came in, and I heard them discovering my lady-- my landlady. And they raped her while I was listening to the whole racket. And afterwards, I stayed almost all the night there, being afraid to go out. And in the morning, when it was so quiet, this Boris [INAUDIBLE] man came, and he said, 'Ania, come out. It's all right.' He came for me, and I came out. The Germans had moved out from that yard. And the Germans stayed, the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: The what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know-- were you clear that the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that, that-- I knew that we were free, free-- whatever, to do whatever. And so afterwards, afterwards, I have met a couple of people who were Polish. And there was one lady who said that she was Jewish. Her name was Anna. And she said that she just had an assumed name and nobody really knew that she was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was working on the farm. And she somehow has gotten the idea-- or got it from somebody-- that in Memmingen, there are-- there is-- it's sort of like a [FRENCH]. What's the word in French? In French? I'm speaking French now. That's OK. In miming, did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Memmingen. Memmingen. That's a city down from [GERMAN], where I was. And we were walking there for-- that was about 80 miles from where I was. We were walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with--

speaker: interviewee
text: With Anna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anna.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came to the gate of that place, of meeting of all the nationalities there, where they were sorted out and sent to their respective countries-- when I came to the gate, I simply fainted. And Anna ran into the inside asking-- to ask for help. And when I came to, there was this gentleman standing over me in a uniform. And he started talking to me in French.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't recog-- didn't undersatnd, so he started speaking in German, a little bit. And he asked me where I was-- where I-- who I was, where I'm going, what for, and I told him. And I said that I want-- he asks me where I want to go. And I say I want to go to France. I want to look for my parents. And he-- [INAUDIBLE] was asking me, who your parents were, what was their names, and what was your father's name? I say, my father's name is Joseph Kosturek. And he stares at me and starts snickering. And he says, Joseph Kosturek? Is he a fireman, by any chance? I said, yes. And he said, well, you're in the right place. God brought you here right to me. I know your father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you believe that? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was Father [INAUDIBLE], who lived in [? Rohan, ?] at a church there, and he [INAUDIBLE]-- he was a chaplain in the French army, and-- Did he take you to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away. He managed to find transport for me with a truck full of French soldiers, POWs, and he asked one of his clerics to take care of me. [LAUGHS] And I came to Lyon and from there to Rohan, where my-- it was-- I remember it was midnight, well past midnight. And apparently somebody phoned the mayor or le maire in [INAUDIBLE], where my father was living. And the mayor phoned my parents and, 'your daughter is here.' They had no idea that I was in the camps, of course. They didn't know where I was. So, come over. I'm coming-- or rather, I'm coming for you, right over, and I'm going to take you to Rohan because your daughter is at the station.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father came. And there were quite a few women, women also who came, mostly the army women. And he was staring at me. It reminded me of-- again of Dachau, where people looked at people, searching who he-- who she is, what she is. And he looked at me. He didn't recognize anybody there. And finally this man, this mayor, maire, says, but look at this one. She is your daughter. She looks exactly like you. What are you looking for? [LAUGHS] So he took me to-- they took me to my place, to my mother's place, directly. And that was the beginning of a new life. What did you do after the war in France, before you came to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had to catch up in my studies, so I studied in a Franco-Polish military high school, and it was accelerated [INAUDIBLE], so in two years' time, I got my baccalaureate. And then I was studying at the University of Grenoble, and also later on I moved to Paris, where I studied at Sorbonne, early French literature. And I also studied to the-- went to the Ecole Superieure de Secretariat near-- across the Pantheon, where I finished the business courses, the secretariat, secretariat courses. And from then on, what happened was another terrible thing. My father was murdered. I have written a novel about it, which is called [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And did I gave it-- I gave it to you. Great. My father was murdered, so I had to take care of my mother. My mother was completely out of it. She was terribly shocked. And I decided then I will go to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was also in some camp, some concentration camp, but he was working on-- in Austria in-- what do you call that where you cut the rocks? He was very, very-- he became very, very sick. And I don't know what kind of miracle or luck-- they threw him in one of the cattle trains that was going to Poland, and they sent him back to Poland. He regained-- he was brought up to life again by his-- his uncle and his aunt. And in-- when I was in Canada in 1960, I brought him to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you and your mother come over to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in '53. My mother came for my wedding in '54.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you still married?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Are you-- are-- you said you got-- you got married. Are you still married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was married until '94. My hus-- my husband died in 1954-- '94, I mean. 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Adam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a survivor also?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a survivor of Siberian camps, and he's also a veteran of Monte Cassino and other battles for freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any children together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have one unique, special girl, Irene Renata Ilona.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's-- I don't know that I should give you that secret away. She might be not too happy about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does she live near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lives near me in the same-- same city. We just separated two years ago. So I wanted to be on my own, and I wanted to give her some space, and I wanted to write. Give my time-- I have more time for writing and painting, drawing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we-- before I ask you some closing questions, did any of your family members perish during the war? Because of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not us directly. Not directly. No, not directly. Not that I know. Maybe somebody in my father's family, but we were not that close with my father's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: My other question is, do you know of any reprisals that were taken because of-- Chaya's escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know much about. I didn't have, to all my staying in Germany, I had no news from Poland. And after the war only, I have learned from a friend of mine-- only after the war somebody wrote me that you were really a hostage for Chaya, and that your uncle sold you for some amount of grain for somebody else. That somebody else was supposed to go, and you went. And that's about-- that's all I know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did your experiences in the war affect your belief in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my belief in God never ceased, since I had so many miraculous escapes, almost near-death instances. And therefore, I have published a book about-- of Polish, Polish religious books-- poems, Polish religious poems, about 400, 400 pages book, which I believe has-- will have some use. And I also have written, among others-- you probably have a taste of my-- taste of my poetry reading about Dachau that you have with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you told your story before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at this length. I've said a few words here and there, but people-- I am rather sorry about it. People don't like to listen about such things. They say, well, it's, it's over, it's gone. Let's go on. Life goes on. Forget about it. It's hard, and even my parents didn't want to listen. Even my husband. He also, who went through a lot, he was not talking much about it. We just took life as it came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you choose to tell your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for the simple reason because Steven Spielberg was advertising on TV that he would be delighted to hear from some other people to-- that went through-- that are survivors, that went through something like I did to recount their story. And so, I don't know. On an impulse, I phoned. And this is the way I came here. And anyway, my daughter wanted me very much to write this story about all my, my experiences in the camps and so on. She wants to have that for her-- for posterity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you had an opportunity to discuss your experiences with other survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I don't find too many people in Guelph that would be discussing this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you part of any-- any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up to now, my husband was very much anti-Polish, for some reason. I don't know all the reason. So I didn't-- before I was teaching Polish schools and also had a Polish radio hour in Guelph. Besides, I don't associate too much-- did not associate too much with Polish people because of that, about my husband's some kind of a complex. And it, it hurts me. And first of all, I am very much suffering through all my life from nostalgia. I would love to see my country again, which I haven't seen for 56 years. I don't know. Maybe I will have eventually enough money somewhere to go there and see or trace the place where I was born, and show, maybe, this place to my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of your book that you're writing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- you wouldn't believe me. I didn't even give it a title yet. I am hesitating about the title. My first title was And then, no. And then, Unextinguished thirst, which means that I was always thirsting for knowledge, and no matter what happened, I was going after it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel that you've had an opportunity to attain it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think not-- not while I was married, because my husband considered that women are for pots and pans and children. So I was doing a lot of things sort of, again, in hiding. And then he was-- he became sick. He was sick for 17 years, not well. And in '92, he was run over by a car, and ended up in-- his brain was damaged. He had a broken skull.

speaker: interviewer
text: He ended up in Homewood here. I don't know whether you've heard of Homewood, where he died. And that was the end of our story. And his. Or is it? We still have a lot of things to cover, but I won't be extending it. Well, we look forward to reading your book and thank you so much for sharing your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you, Aviva. And I hope that this would serve. Especially, I would love to give homage to all of the admirable heroes of Jewish origin, because they were the real heroes and the greatest sufferers in this war. And I hope that their nation will survive and will go to the height that they expect to go. I believe they are quite a courageous nation. Quite courageous. What I have seen, it's beyond description.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you for sharing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always admired the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your courage with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And if there was any part that was-- I know there were a lot of various people in my Polish nation that were against the Jewish people. And I am very sorry for it. I wish-- if I were the one to atone for it, I would. But there is no atonement for many mistakes that were made. Thank you. On the right side, you see my father, Joseph Kosturek, who was born in Poland and is not alive anymore. On the-- on the left side, extreme left, is my mother, Maria-- Maria Kosturek, born Wlazlow. And in the middle, up high, is my brother Augustyn, who was born in 1924. He's not alive anymore. And at the bottom is myself, [INAUDIBLE], who is in the outfit for the First Communion. This picture was taken-- really, it's a composite of many pictures, of four pictures that I put together and gave it to an artist in Paris, who made this picture for me. My brother-- my brother's picture was taken in thirty-- in 1933. And my own picture was taken when I was seven, and that is in 1934. And this is my father's picture. My father's picture or my mother's pictures must have been done before the war, and I don't remember when. This is the picture of my husband, Adam Radek, who was born in 1916. And this picture was taken while he was passed to the battle of Monte Cassino in 1944 in Italy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And this is in Paris, where I studied. And here I am with my diploma in hand, walking in front of the [INAUDIBLE] du Carrousel. This is shortly-- I am here shortly after my coming to my parents' farm. I am in a poetic mood, somewhere against the rocky elevation. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the Arbeitskarte, or work card, which was given by [SPEAKING GERMAN] authorities when I came there. And it's called Arbeitskarte Bescheinigung for the Lohnersparnisse, which means that apparently I am supposed to get some money, which I never did. And below is my picture with the German stamps. And as you see, I have been given this picture much later on the 14th of April, 1943. And I have the shaven head, because I came from the camp of Dachau. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the Arbeitskarte again, on the other side, where my name is written, as you see, and also some other jotting, which they are telling us where-- when I came in there. And you have a seal and the stamp of the German high and mighty Reich. And this is my daughter, Irene Renata Radek. She is the best daughter in the world, and I am very proud of her and all her achievements.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother has taught me a lot about life. I am an only child, so our relationship was-- was closer, even, than most families could be, most mother and daughter relationships would be. And I learned a lot from my mother about survival. That's one thing that I've always known about my mother, that no matter how tough things got, she would survive and she would see it through. And she instilled that in me as well. Sometimes it's difficult for me to look at her and realize that she's gone through all the things that she has gone through. Right now I'm helping my mom work on her book. I'm editing what she has written. And sometimes it's very difficult to read that and realize that my mother, the lady I'm sitting beside here, the lady that I've spent my life with, has gone through such horrendous things. But in another way, it's a great inspiration to me because I know that if she could get through those things and still remain so good-natured and good-humored, with always a smile on her face and a dance in her step, that she has taught me well to get through anything that I may face as well. And I want to thank you, Mom, for everything, and I love you very much. Thank you.
